coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots in ionic liquid as metal free catalyst for highly selective production of hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Pham-Truong, Thuan-Nguyen
Petenzi, Thomas
Ranjan, Christine
Randriamahazaka, Hyacinthe
Ghilane, Jalal
coredata.dc:description: Nanometer scale doped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using the microwave assisted method in the presence of bio-based materials and ionic liquid (1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate). First, glutamine and glucose in ionic liquid were used as carbon and nitrogen source. The surface characterizations confirm the formation of N-doped carbon dots with the presence of imidazolium and ionic liquid anion at the extreme surface. Next to that, CDs were generated in the absence of glutamine while using the ionic liquid as source of nitrogen. The TEM and surface analysis confirm the formation of nanoscale N-doped carbon dots. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of the generated CDs towards the oxygen reduction were investigated. As a result, both CDs materials exhibit a pronounced electrocatalytic activity with a prevalent 2 electron pathway leading to the formation of hydrogen peroxide. More interestingly, the CDs generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid display a selective 2 electron reduction of O2, with H2O2 production higher than 90% for a potential range up to 0.8 V (from 0.6 V to −0.2 V vs RHE).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microwave assisted synthesis
Bio-based materials
Carbon dots
Ionic liquid
Electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and characterization

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Centre National de la Recherche Scientifique

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge support from the CNRS and doctoral school (ED388). The authors thank Dr. Philippe Decorse for performing the XPS measurements and for the valuable discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic pathway of Carbon dots in ionic liquids media (B) Samples at different baking temperature.
(A) FT-IR spectrum of CDs-1 (B) XPS survey spectrum of CDs-1, (C) and (D) high resolution XPS spectra of C1s and N1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(A) FT-IR spectrum of CDs-2 (B) XPS survey spectrum of CDs-2, (C) high resolution XPS spectra of C1s. (D) XPS of N1s and inset XPS of S2p. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
TEM image of CDs-2 and the corresponding particle size distribution. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Scheme illustrating the RRDE setup and the ORR polarization curves, in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution, using RRDE at rotation 1600 r.p.m with 10 mV/s scan rate for CDs-1 synthesized at different temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) ORR polarization curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution using RRDE of CDs-1 sample generated at 170 °C at different rotations speeds. (B) Koutecky-Levich plots at different potentials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Variation of the electron number and the hydrogen peroxide percentage as function of the potential for CDs-1 sample. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) ORR polarization curves in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution using RDE at rotation 1600 r.p.m with 10 mV/s scan rate of bare GC (black line) and CDs-2 sample (red line). (B) ORR polarization curves using RRDE of CDs-2 sample generated at 200 °C at different rotations speeds. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Variation of the electron number and the hydrogen peroxide percentage as function of the potential for CDs-2 sample. (B). Chronoamperometric response of CDs-2 RDE relative current variation at 0.2 V as a function of time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The ORR parameters obtained on different CDs and the comparison with the literature.

head.ce:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots in ionic liquid as metal free catalyst for highly selective production of hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université Paris Diderot, Sorbonne Paris Cité, ITODYS, UMR 7086 CNRS, SIELE Group, 15 rue Jean-Antoine de Baïf, 75013, Paris, France

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Université Paris Diderot, Sorbonne Paris Cité, ITODYS, UMR 7086 CNRS, SIELE Group, 15 rue Jean-Antoine de Baïf, 75013, Paris, France.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanometer scale doped carbon dots (CDs) were prepared using the microwave assisted method in the presence of bio-based materials and ionic liquid (1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate). First, glutamine and glucose in ionic liquid were used as carbon and nitrogen source. The surface characterizations confirm the formation of N-doped carbon dots with the presence of imidazolium and ionic liquid anion at the extreme surface. Next to that, CDs were generated in the absence of glutamine while using the ionic liquid as source of nitrogen. The TEM and surface analysis confirm the formation of nanoscale N-doped carbon dots. Furthermore, the catalytic activity of the generated CDs towards the oxygen reduction were investigated. As a result, both CDs materials exhibit a pronounced electrocatalytic activity with a prevalent 2 electron pathway leading to the formation of hydrogen peroxide. More interestingly, the CDs generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid display a selective 2 electron reduction of O2, with H2O2 production higher than 90% for a potential range up to 0.8 V (from 0.6 V to −0.2 V vs RHE).

ce:section.ce:para: Presenting as one of the most abundant elements and importantly contributing to the life on our planet, oxygen acts as a crucial component for numerous systems. Therefore, chemistry of oxygen, especially the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), has been widely studied for several applications including energy storage and conversion to environmental issues [1,2]. Even though the mechanism of the oxygen reduction reaction is still unclear due to the complexity of system [3]. However, water and hydrogen peroxide have been identified as the main products via 4-electron and 2-electron reduction pathways, respectively [4]. The electro-reduction of oxygen to water has been widely investigated for regenerative fuel cells [5,6] and metal-air batteries [7]. However, the reduction process does not always conduct to the formation of water. In general, the electron exchanged number is usually lower than four, marking the production of the second main products, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [8,9]. In the literature, the production of H2O2 is less studied than the 4-electron pathway. However, hydrogen peroxide has been listed among the 100 most important chemical products [10] and the compound contributes to different applications from laboratory manipulations [11,12], industrial productions [13] to daily life's products [14,15].

Various methods have been proposed for generating hydrogen peroxide since the beginning of the last century. The annual production of hydrogen peroxide exceeds a few million tons. Actually, the hydrogen peroxide is produced based on the anthraquinone process in a large scale which includes the sequential hydrogenation and oxidation of anthraquinone [16]. Nevertheless, the complexity of the process along with the explosion risk stimulates the development of an alternative process [17,18]. Recently, electrochemical generation of hydrogen peroxide from the oxygen reduction or the water evolution using a catalyst has been investigated to reduce the previously mentioned problems [19]. The presence of a catalyst becomes crucial for this approach by lowering the overpotential of the electrochemical reactions which induces a decrease of the energy costs. Electro-catalysts for ORR via two-electron pathway can be classified into 3 main categories, which are noble metals and its alloys, non-precious metals and metal free catalysts [2,20,21,22,23]. Palladium modified gold nanoparticles is the most active catalyst for hydrogen peroxide production [17].

Based on the most important element on Earth, carbon based materials appear to be a “greener” approach for resolving the large-scale production of hydrogen peroxide. Carbon based nanomaterials (carbon nanotube arrays, graphene, doped graphene, etc.) have been widely studied as highly efficient catalyst for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [24]. Recent studies on microwave assisted method demonstrate a supporting evidence for the formation of carbon quantum dots (CDs) from bio-abundant starting materials such as glucose and amino acids [25]. Possessing the same functions as a household microwave, the laboratory microwave reactor provides a faster synthesis than conventional hydro/solvo-thermal methods with higher product yield. As reported in previous works, the formation of CDs can only occur at high temperature (>160 °C), the synthesis of these nanoparticles requires a novel solvent that withstands at high temperature. Owning a unique physical-chemical properties, the ionic liquid offers a large temperature range for synthetizing CDs. Although there have been important research focusing on the microwave assisted synthesis of doped carbon dots, their use in electrocatalysis towards oxygen reduction is still scanty.

In the present work, the synthesis of carbon dots were performed in ionic liquid, 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate, using the microwave assisted solvo-thermal method. The CDs were generated from glucose/glutamine dissolved in ionic liquid or glucose in ionic liquid. The generated carbon materials were characterized by several surface sensitive techniques. The as-prepared CDs display a nanometer size particles and N-doping with the presence of thin layer of the ionic liquid. Next, the electrocatalytic activity of the CDs toward the oxygen reduction reaction was investigated. The CDs prepared in the presence of ionic liquid exhibit a preferential oxygen reduction reaction through hydrogen peroxide pathway. More interestingly, the CDs generated from glucose/ionic liquid display a selective two-electron oxygen reduction with a high efficiency of hydrogen peroxide production.

d-Glucose (>99%), l-Glutamine (>99%), 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate (>95%), sodium borohydride (98%), potassium hydroxide (>99%) and Nafion® 117 solution were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the chemical products were used as received.

The syntheses of different CDs were carried out in a monomode microwave reactor (Anton Paar 300). Typically, 1.4 g of glucose and 0.7 g of glutamine were dissolved under vigorous stirring in 20 ml of 1-ethyl-3-methylimidazolium ethylsulfate ([EImM+][ESO4 -]) or in 20 ml of distilled water. Then the mixture was poured into a special-purpose vial for heating via microwave irradiation. The temperature ramp was constantly kept at 1.5 °C/s. Different CDs samples were obtained using different baking temperatures. After 5 min at desired temperature, the solution was cooled down to room temperature followed by dialysis using cellulose membrane (Spectra/Por® Dialysis Tubing with MWCO = 7 kDa) for 48 h. Finally, the dialyzed suspensions of CDs were centrifuged at 800 rpm for 20 min to eliminate non-desired agglomerations and the supernatants were collected resulting in yellowish solutions and were ready for further uses. The CDs generated in ionic liquid were labeled CDs-1 and those synthesized in water were labeled CDs-3.

A similar procedure was used for the synthesis of other CDs in the absence of glutamine. Briefly, 1.4 g of glucose was dissolved in 20 ml of [EImM+][ESO4 -] (CDs-2) or in 20 ml of distilled water (CDs-4). Then, the solutions were heated with the microwave assisted method for 5 min at controlled temperatures with a temperature ramp at 10 °C/min. Once the solutions were cooled down to ambient, the CDs solutions were purified by dialysis (MWCO = 7 kDa) followed by centrifugation as previously described.

XPS measurements of the particles were performed using a Thermo VG Scientific ESCALAB 250 system fitted with a microfocused, monochromatic Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) 200 W X-ray source. Infrared spectra were obtained using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) via a Thermoscientific (Nicolet 8700). The particles size distribution of the as-prepared carbon dots were obtained via TEM image using a JEOL JEM 100CX.

Before being used as working electrode, the glassy carbon was carefully polished using different alumina pastes (grain size ranging from 2 μm to 0.3 μm). Then the electrode was rinsed thoroughly with distilled water before being treated with a 0.1 M solution of sodium borohydride for 10 min. Again, the pre-treated electrode was rinsed with water and served for further measurements. The catalytic ink for electrochemical measurements was prepared as follows. 200 μL of purified suspension of carbon dots was mixed with 500 μL of absolute ethanol followed by the addition of 25 μL of Nafion® 117 solution. The as-prepared mixture was sonicated for 3 times 10 min. Finally, 10 μL of the solution was drop-cast onto the glassy carbon part of a rotating disk electrode. After drying at ambient temperature, the catalytic film was homogeneously formed on the electrode and was used for further electrochemical measurements.

For electrochemical measurements, a rotating ring disk setup was used. A mercury-mercury oxide electrode (Hg/HgO 1 M KOH) and a stainless-steel mesh were used as a reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. A rotating ring disk electrode (E7R9 series) from Pine Research Instruments was used for the electrochemical measurements which are controlled with a CHI920C bipotentiostat (CH Instruments, Austin, Texas). For ORR measurements, 0.1 M KOH solution was used as an electrolyte and bubbled with highly purified oxygen for 30 min. During electrocatalytic performance's tests, the as-prepared solution was kept under oxygen atmosphere. All the potentials reported in this work were converted to a reversible hydrogen electrode system (RHE).

For evaluating the catalytic performances, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was used. The LSV measurements were launched at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 for a large rotation speed ranging from 400 to 2500 rpm. From the obtained LSVs, the cross point of the tangents derived from the i-V curve just before and after the rise of the catalytic current is defined as the onset potential (Eonset). For measuring the catalytic performance the following formulas (1–4) were used.
(1)
1 j
=
1
j k
+
1
j
lev
=A+
1
B .
ω
1/2
(2)
B=0 .2nF
v
-1/6
D
3/2
C
o 2
=0 .0355n
where j, jk and jlev are the current density extracted from LSV results, the calculated kinetic current density and the corresponding diffusion controlled current density. The diffusion controlled current is related to the Faraday constant (F), 96500 C mol−1; the kinetic viscosity of the medium (υ), 0.01 cm2 s−1; the oxygen diffusion coefficient (D), 1.9.10−5 cm2 s−1; the oxygen concentration is 1.2.10−6 mol cm−3 and the rotation speed of the electrode (ω) and the constant 0.2 is used when the rotation speed (ω) is expressed in rounds per minute (rpm). The kinetic current is obtained by extrapolating the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plot (j−1/2 vs ω−1/2) at different rotation speeds and B could be determined from corresponding K-L slope.

Besides, the number of exchanged electron number and the quantity of hydrogen peroxide formed during the reduction process were estimated from the equations (3–4):
(3)
4
I D
I D

I R

(4)

where ID is the faradic disk current, IR is the corresponding faradic ring current and N = 0.37 is the collecting efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped carbon dots were synthesized as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The as-prepared CDs are highly soluble in water and other polar solvents, such as ethanol, dimethylformamide.

To investigate the chemical composition of the prepared CDs FT-IR and XPS analyses were performed. First, the microwave assisted synthesis of CDs-1 from a mixture of glucose (3.5%) and glutamine (7%) in the presence of ionic liquid at 170 °C during 5 min were characterized. The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2 A) shows a broad peak at 3300 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibration of O—H groups. The peaks at 2920, 2850 and 750 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of aromatic and aliphatic C—H [26]. Besides that, the CO stretching vibrations were observed at 1665 and 1600 cm−1 [27]. The peaks located at 1220 and 990 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of C—N group [28] The FT-IR analysis reveal the presence of imidazole or imidazolium structure suggesting that the latter remain on the surface. These results confirm the formation of N doped CDs in the ionic liquid media. Fig. 2B shows the XPS survey of the CDs-1 and reveals the presence of three main components corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s.

The deconvolution of the C(1s) high resolution spectra (Fig. 2C) exhibits the presence of 4 components at the binding energies of 285, 286.1, 287.5 and 288.5 eV which can be attributed to the binding energy of carbon atom derived from the C backbone (C-C), amide (C-N), –CN group from imidazolium ring and –COO, respectively. The π–π* satellite peak around 292.5 eV characteristic of conjugated systems is observed. The N(1s) signal, Fig. 2D, shows two components at 398.8 and 401.5 eV attributed to pyridinic and graphitic like N, respectively [29]. The absence of peak at 405 eV, corresponding to N—O groups, suggests that the N are mainly linked to C or H atoms [30]. The total atomic percentage of N element, in CDs-1, is around 8% corresponding to a pyridinic-N (6%) and a graphitic-N (2%). The XPS analysis reveals also the presence of peak at 168 eV assigned to sulfate originated from ionic liquid anion. The existence of sulfate signal suggests the presence of imidazolium on the extreme surface of the generated CDs [31]. Overall, the above results confirm the formation of N doped CDs-1 generated through microwave route in the presence of bio-based carbon source (glucose) and nitrogen source (glutamine) in ionic liquids.

The imidazolium based ionic liquids contain nitrogen group within the imidazolium ring. The purpose of the next part is to investigate the capability of imidazolium ionic liquid to be a source of nitrogen in the microwave assisted synthesis of N doped CDs. To this end, CDs-2 were synthesized in a solution containing glucose and ionic liquid. Similar procedure as described above was followed. Briefly, 7 g of glucose (7 % wt.) were added into 89.5 g of 1-ethyl-3-methyl imidazolium ethyl sulfate (IL) solution. Under microwave irradiation, the solution was heated at different temperatures (ranging from 100 to 220 °C) that was maintained for 5 min.

The generated CDs-2 (at 200 °C) were characterized by FT-IR and XPS as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The FT-IR displays the same characteristics as those reported in Fig. 2A, thus the peaks corresponding to the O—H, C—H, CO, and C—N were observed. In addition, a peak at 1480 cm−1 corresponding to CC stretching vibration is detected highlighting that the CDs-2 contain more conjugated structure and a peak at 1660 cm−1 related to the CN vibration is observed.

The XPS survey spectrum shown in Fig. 3B displays the presence of three main components C1s, N1s and O1s. The deconvolution of the C(1s) high resolution spectra shows a four components corresponding to C—C, C—N, CO and O—CO bonds (Fig. 3C). However, compared to the spectrum in Fig. 2C (CDs-1) the contribution of the C—N bond is higher than the C—C bonds. This result is confirmed within the N1s where the predominant peak is observed at 401.5 eV corresponding to graphitic like N, while the contribution of the pyridinic like N is strongly decreased (Fig. 3D). The total atomic percentage of N element, in CDs-2, is around 8% corresponding to a pyridinic-N (0.9%) and a graphitic-N (7.1%). For both synthesized CDs (1 and 2) similar total amount of nitrogen is observed, however, the main nitrogen component in CDs-2 is graphitic-N (7.1%), while for CDs-1 the main component is pyridinic-N (6%). Besides that, the S2p peak at 168 eV is observed (inset Fig. 3D) with an atomic percentage around 4%, suggesting the presence of sulfate and thus the presence of the ionic liquid anion.

Fig. 4 displays the TEM image of the CDs-2 generated in the presence of glucose in ionic liquid at 200 °C during 5 min. The synthesized CDs-2 exhibit almost a spherical shape with particles size distribution in the range of 12 ± 2 nm. All these results confirm the possibility to generate N doped CDs using ionic liquid as N source.

The oxygen reduction reaction performance of the as-prepared CDs were investigated. First, the CDs-1 generated from a mixture of glucose and glutamine in ionic liquid at different temperatures were tested using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE). The CDs were mixed in nafion and deposited onto RRDE electrode. Fig. 5 shows the LSV of various CDs-1 (obtained at different temperatures) performed in 0.1 M O2-saturated KOH solution at 1600 rpm with 10 mV/s scan rate.

The LSV curves show clearly that the N doped CDs-1 exhibits pronounced ORR electrocatalytic activity when compared to bare GC. The curves show two reduction process, suggesting the occurrence of the ORR through consecutive two electron steps with the generation of peroxides as an intermediate. In addition, the CDs-1 sample prepared at 170 °C displays the best performance towards the ORR as attested by the more positive onset potential and higher current density. For the CDs-1 samples prepared at higher temperature the ORR catalytic activity is less pronounced compared to the sample prepared at 170 °C. In order to get more insight about the performance of the CDs-1 towards ORR, the electrochemical investigations were complemented by performing the LSV on RRDE at various rotation rates (Fig. 6 ). The selected sample is the CDs-1 prepared using glucose/glutamine in ionic liquid at 170 °C.

Fig. 6A displays the observed current at the disk and ring electrode showing two reduction waves. From the LSV results the Koutecky-Levich (K-L) plot was performed as shown in Fig. 5B. Linear variation of j−1 and ω−1/2 was observed at different potentials from 0.3 to −0.2 V vs RHE suggesting a first order reaction. The value of the transferred electron number n varies from 2.25 to 2.15 within the potential range 0.3 to −0.2 V (Fig. 6B). These values suggest that the ORR catalyzed by the N-doped CDs-1 occurs preferentially via two electron transfer pathway.

Next, the electrochemical activity of the CDs-1 for oxygen reduction to produce hydrogen peroxide was quantified using the RRDE measurements. The ring potential was fixed at 1.2 V where the generated hydrogen peroxide, at the disk, oxidizes under mass transport control. Thus, the ring current shows a positive current due to the oxidation of H2O2. Fig. 7 displays the variation of the transferred electron transfer and the efficiency of hydrogen peroxide production as a function of the potential.

The curve shows clearly that the transferred electron number remains stable at a 2.15 within the potential range 0.7 to 0.2 V, next it increases slightly to reach a value of 2.25 in the potential region between 0 and −0.2 V. For the H2O2 production, the efficiency is as high as 92% in the potential region from 0.7 to 0.3 V, and decreases slightly in the region 0.2 to −0.2 V reaching a value of 87%. These results confirm that the oxygen reduction on CDs-1 catalyst occurs through the 2 electron process with a selective and high production of hydrogen peroxide over a wide range of potential.

Next, the electrochemical catalytic activity of CDs-2 generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid under microwave at 200 °C during 5 min was investigated. Fig. 8 shows the electrochemical response of the CDs-2 towards the ORR.

Fig. 8A shows the LSV response of the CDs-2 in 0.1 M O2-saturated KOH solution at 1600 rpm with 10 mV/s scan rate. Compared to bare CG, the CDs-2 displays higher catalytic activity as attested by the increased onset potential and higher current density. The curve exhibits two reduction waves, suggesting the occurrence of the ORR through consecutive two electron steps with the generation of peroxides as intermediate. Fig. 8 B shows the LSV curves recorded on RRDE at different rotation rates. The disk current shows a negative current related to the oxygen reduction, while the ring current exhibits a positive current attributed to the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide generated at the disk electrode. The onset potential observed in the ring and disk electrodes overlap at 0.68 V and the current density of both electrodes increase with the enhancement of the rotation speed. Fig. 9 A reports the variation of the transferred electron number and the efficiency of hydrogen peroxide production as a function of the potential.

The transferred electron number is 2.05 for a potential range from 0.7 to 0.3 V, while it increases for potential lower than 0.3 V and reach 2.18 at −0.2 V. These values are slightly lower than that observed in Fig. 7 suggesting that the CD's generated in the absence of glutamine are more favorable for hydrogen peroxide production. Furthermore, the efficiency of H2O2 production reaches a value of 95% and remain at this value for a potential ranging from 0.7 to 0.3 V. Next, in the region 0.2 to −0.2 V the %H2O2 decreases slightly and reach an efficiency of about 91%. The stability of the catalyst was tested by recording the relative current variation as a function of time at a constant potential 0.2 V during 10000 s (Fig. 9B). During the first 1000 s the CDs-2 shows a slight decreases in the relative current to 95% and then stabilize at this value for a long period up to 10000 s. Overall, the N-doped CD's generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid exhibit outstanding performance towards the production of H2O2 up to 90% over large potential range. The selectivity and the high efficiency of the CDs generated in ionic liquid for the hydrogen peroxide production could be explained by their chemical composition. Indeed, the catalytic properties of the CDs could be linked to the presence of nitrogen (electron-rich dopant) in the imidazolium structure which generate a high electron density and provide a positive charge density of carbon atoms. Furthermore, the presence of imidazolium cation and sulfate anion at the extreme CDs surface could participate in the enhancement of oxygen solubility, favor the oxygen adsorption and participate in the release of intermediates species through the electrostatic interaction.

In order to confirm the role of the ionic liquid during the synthesis, CDs using either a mixture of glucose and glutamine or only glucose in aqueous solution were synthesized under the microwave and denoted as CDs-3 and CDs-4, respectively. The electrochemical oxygen reduction performances were reported on Table 1 and compared to CDs generated in ionic liquid.

As shown in Table 1, the CDs-3 and CDs-4 generated in the presence of glucose and glutamine or glucose in H2O display onset potential similar to that reported for the CDs-1 and CDs-2 generated in ionic liquids. However, the transferred electron number is much higher close to 2.5 and the maximum efficiency of hydrogen peroxide is 85% and 75% for N-doped CDs-3 and CDs-4, respectively. This result confirms the important role of imidazolium based ionic liquid during the synthesis of the N-doped CDs and their selectivity towards hydrogen peroxide production. In addition, compared to the literature the N doped CDs generated in ionic liquid shows higher H2O2 production when compared to other carbon based catalysts, including mesoporous N doped carbon and PtHg/C nanoparticles [32-33,34,35,36,37,38]. More interestingly, the CDs-2 generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid exhibits H2O2 production higher than 90% over a potential range up to 0.8 V.

In summary, carbon dots were successfully synthesized by means of microwave assisted methods using bio-based materials and ionic liquid. The glucose and glutamine in ionic liquid as starting material leads to the formation of nitrogen doped carbon dots. Surface analysis confirms the presence of pyridinic and graphitic nitrogen and also imidazolium and ionic liquid anion at the CDs surface. In addition, the nitrogen source (glutamine) was successfully replaced by the nitrogen of the ionic liquid within the imidazolium ring. In this case, the generated CDs exhibits only the presence of graphitic nitrogen. The synthesized CDs were tested towards the oxygen reduction reaction. As a result, both CDs show a promising activity towards the ORR with high selectivity towards the hydrogen peroxide production. The CDs generated in the presence of glucose and ionic liquid exhibit outstanding performance and selectivity for the ORR through the 2 electron pathway with an efficiency of H2O2 production up to 90% over a large potential range 0.8 V. This work not only demonstrates the possibility to generate nanoscale doped carbon dots in ionic liquid, but also provides an efficient metal-free catalyst for H2O2 production.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge support from the CNRS and doctoral school (ED388). The authors thank Dr. Philippe Decorse for performing the XPS measurements and for the valuable discussions.
